Season in progress · Round 5 of 11 complete

Five rounds into the season, the championship is beginning to reveal its true shape but behind the surging Walkenhorst squad, the fight remains as unpredictable as ever.

A backyard 1:32 endurance series that's turned into a genuine championship fight patience vs. pace vs. chaos, with the track itself occasionally joining the race.

The State of Play

Race Control Bulletin
Five rounds into the championship, identities are finally beginning to emerge, but questions still remain about who can sustain their form over the long season ahead.

Engstler entered Sebring as a comfortable points leader built on discipline and consistency — they left it tied for 4th after their worst result of the year. Walkenhorst, who looked like a nice trend-line story at best entering Round 3, is now the outright championship leader after the wildest individual race day of anyone in the field. That's a full changing-of-the-guard, and it happened in a single round.

Fourteen cars, fourteen identities

Team by team

Every entry's form so far, distilled into one line — from the points leader to the team still looking for their first big result.

Race Control Bulletin
Five rounds into the championship, identities are finally beginning to emerge, but questions still remain about who can sustain their form over the long season ahead.

Engstler entered Sebring as a comfortable points leader built on discipline and consistency — they left it tied for 4th after their worst result of the year. Walkenhorst, who looked like a nice trend-line story at best entering Round 3, is now the outright championship leader after the wildest individual race day of anyone in the field. That's a full changing-of-the-guard, and it happened in a single round.

Round 1 · Complete

Daytona — 24 Hours

The championship opened with its hardest test right out of the gate: 24 hours, where track position means nothing at 2 AM and everything at sunrise.

Full Finishing Order

PosCarTeamPoints

The Story

Engstler Motorsport's No. 8 Audi ran the race everybody says they want to run and almost nobody actually executes — conservative fuel windows, no heroics in traffic, tire management that kept them out of trouble during the overnight stint when fatigue wrecks fields. Never the fastest car on a hot lap, but the one car that wasn't in the recovery garage at 4 AM. Textbook 24-hour execution, and it's exactly why they left Florida with a championship lead nobody predicted.

Northwest Racing's No. 98 was the real surprise — no real form line coming in, but they caught a favorable strategy window late in the race and never gave it back for a runner-up finish.

Schubert Motorsport's No. 31 did what would become their signature all season — no drama, no story, just a clean, disciplined run to third.

Further down, Ford Performance's No. 64 (4th) and Red Bull's No. 30 (5th) quietly banked solid, unspectacular days. Corvette Racing's No. 3 (6th) and Walkenhorst's No. 36 (7th) had similarly workmanlike runs.

And then there's the pain. ABT's No. 07 salvaged 8th after a difficult overnight stretch, while sister car No. 7 could only manage 11th — the first hint of a gap between two cars from the same shop that would only grow.

At the bottom, it was brutal for two cars that would matter a lot later. Team Bernhard's No. 75 struggled to 13th, and AlphaTauri's No. 23 never found a rhythm, finishing 12th, while JP-Motorsport's No. 15 was dead last. Both clearly quick over a single lap. Neither had a clue yet how to survive 24 hours of racing.

Round 2 · Complete

Laguna Seca — 6 Hours

A completely different animal. A 6-hour sprint doesn't forgive a slow start the way a 24-hour race does — but it doesn't punish raw aggression either.

Full Finishing Order

PosCarTeamPoints

The Story

AlphaTauri's No. 23 flipped the narrative completely and won going away. A shorter race meant less exposure to whatever cost them at Daytona and a lot more emphasis on the one thing they clearly have in abundance: outright pace. JP-Motorsport's No. 15 told an almost identical story, rocketing from last to a strong runner-up finish.

TF-Sport's No. 33 posted its best result of the season in third, and Walkenhorst's No. 36 backed it up in fourth — both signs the midfield has real speed when the format favors it. Schubert's No. 31 did it again in fifth.

Daytona's big winner came back to earth here. Engstler's No. 8 could only manage sixth — still points on the board, but a sprint race doesn't play to a strategy built around patience and attrition.

Schubert Racing Development's No. 10 picked up seventh, and the ABT gap kept widening — No. 07 scored eighth again while teammate No. 7 finished dead last, a second straight disaster turning into a genuine pattern. Red Bull, Ford, Corvette, Northwest, and Team Bernhard rounded out a much quieter day for the teams that had shone or struggled at Daytona.

Round 3 · Complete — Called at Hour 8 of 12

Sebring — 12 Hours

The single most storied venue in American endurance racing. A track built on a WWII airfield, and the race that flipped the championship on its head.

The race was called at the 8-hour mark for a legitimate real-world reason — the organizer became ill during the event and was later diagnosed with multifocal pneumonia. The result stands as an official finish at 8 hours.

The Track

Sebring isn't just old, it's the original — America's oldest continuously operating road course, built on the runways and service roads of Hendricks Army Airfield, a WWII base that trained B-17 bomber pilots before Alec Ulmann turned it into a race track in 1950. The 12 Hours dates back to 1952, older than Daytona's 24-hour race, and long called even tougher on cars and drivers than Le Mans.

Large sections of the modern 3.74-mile, 17-turn layout still run over the original WWII concrete runway slabs — the seams between them are notoriously rough, sparks fly from underneath the cars, and the bumps have ended races for decades. It felt fitting that the CEC's own patio circuit had its own version of that story in miniature: track joints that shift and complain in the summer heat.

7 Things to Know Going In

01
The championship's real tiebreaker. Three teams, three identities — discipline, consistency, and speed — all forced to answer the same question at once.
02
Engstler's lead isn't the cushion it looks like. 590–560–510 through the top three. One bad pit call reshuffles this by tomorrow night.
03
Schubert might be the scariest team in the field. Zero wins, zero disasters — the exact recipe a long, brutal format could finally convert into an outright win.
04
AlphaTauri is the whole storyline of the season. 12th at Daytona, winner at Laguna — Sebring decides which version is real.
05
Watch the clock, not just the sun. Smooth build to majority-baked by 3–5 PM, cooling out by 8 PM.
06
The ABT garage. Ninety points separating identical machinery — two straight disasters stops being bad luck.
07
The midfield is wide open. Walkenhorst, TF-Sport, Northwest, Ford, and JP-Motorsport separated by 50 points.

Race Recap — Hour by Hour

Final Result called at hour 8

PosCarTeamPoints

Walkenhorst Motorsport wins Round 3. After the most turbulent individual race of the season — early contact, two separate stints at the lead, two drops from it, and finally two full hours holding station clean — the win moves them from fourth in points entering the round to the outright championship lead.

Both pre-race title favorites had rough rounds: Engstler finished 12th, its worst result of the season, and AlphaTauri finished 5th after recovering from as low as 13th at halfway. Corvette Racing's #3 had one of the most notable individual arcs of the day — contact damage, repair, full retirement, an unexpected return, and a climb to 8th by race end.

Round 4 · Complete — 100-Minute Sprint

Long Beach

The endurance grind gives way to the shortest race of the season so far — a tight, unforgiving street circuit with almost no room to recover from a mistake.

The Track

Long Beach is the longest-running major street race in North America, dating back to 1975 — a temporary circuit carved out of the city streets around the old Long Beach Convention Center, running along the waterfront with the signature Shoreline Drive front straight lined by palm trees. The modern layout is 1.968 miles with 11 turns, and it's a genuine dual-purpose venue — host to IndyCar's showcase street race every April, but also a real IMSA SportsCar Championship round in its own right.

7 Things to Know Going In

01
The championship arrives compressed. After Daytona, Laguna Seca, and Sebring, nobody has managed to completely break away. Long Beach is short, chaotic, and could completely reset the title picture before the season reaches its halfway point.
02
Walkenhorst finally has a target on its back. Leading the standings after Sebring, they now have to prove they can win under pressure instead of chasing from behind.
03
Qualifying may matter more here than anywhere else. Long Beach's narrow walls and limited passing zones mean clean air could be worth more than outright pace.
04
The Audi armada is waking up. Engstler, ABT, and Bernhard have all showed flashes of speed recently. If all three hit the setup window at once, the rest of the field could have a serious problem.
05
JP-Motorsport cannot afford another quiet weekend. Consistency has kept them near the front, but championships eventually require wins.
06
Watch for surprise contenders. Teams like TF Sport, Northwest AMR, and Ford Performance may not have title momentum yet, but street circuits have a habit of producing strange podiums.
07
One mistake is magnified here. Unlike Daytona or Sebring, there is almost no time to recover from a crash, missed lane change, or poor pit sequence. Long Beach rewards precision more than outright aggression.

Race Recap

Final Result

PosCarTeamPoints

Walkenhorst Motorsport wins Round 4. After a chaotic opening half of the race — an early fight for the lead, a major incident for the pole-winning Red Bull Ferrari, a full-course yellow, and a dramatic pit cycle reshuffling the field — the #36 Aston Martin emerged with the lead and controlled the final stages to secure their second consecutive victory of the season.

The result continues a remarkable turnaround for Walkenhorst, who entered the season's first sprint race as championship leaders and left Long Beach with an even stronger position heading into Detroit. AlphaTauri's #23 Ferrari delivered another strong recovery drive to finish 2nd, keeping their championship challenge alive, while ABT's #07 Audi completed the podium after a difficult opening to the weekend.

The biggest storyline of the race was the contrast between early pace and final results. Engstler Motorsport's #8 Audi looked like one of the fastest cars in the opening stages, taking the lead and controlling the race early, but the pit cycle dropped them down the order and they ultimately finished 11th. Meanwhile, the #30 Red Bull Ferrari went from controlling the opening laps from the front to a race-ending incident that changed the entire complexion of the event.

Round 5 · Complete — 100-Minute Sprint

Detroit

With momentum building and the championship tightening, Detroit becomes a test of patience, precision, and execution on one of the most demanding temporary circuits of the season.

The Track

Detroit is one of the most historic street circuits in North American motorsport, with its racing history dating back to 1982 when Formula 1 first brought a world championship event to the streets of downtown Detroit. The modern Detroit Grand Prix layout runs through the heart of the city along the Detroit River, creating a tight and technical temporary circuit surrounded by the city's skyline and waterfront.

The current IMSA layout is 1.645 miles with 10 turns, featuring a mix of short straights, heavy braking zones, and narrow street sections that leave little room for error. Unlike traditional permanent circuits, Detroit rewards precision and consistency, with track position often proving more valuable than outright pace.

A fixture of the IMSA SportsCar Championship schedule, Detroit has become known for unpredictable sprint races where strategy, caution periods, and avoiding contact can determine the outcome. With only 100 minutes of racing, teams must maximize every opportunity as there is limited time to recover from mistakes.

7 Things to Know Going In

01
Walkenhorst arrives as the championship benchmark. After consecutive victories at Sebring and Long Beach, the #36 Aston Martin enters Detroit with momentum and a growing points advantage. The question is whether anyone can finally stop the current championship leader. 02
02
Detroit creates a completely different challenge. Like Long Beach, Detroit is a short sprint event, but the layout presents its own challenges. Track position, qualifying performance, and avoiding mistakes will be crucial with limited time to recover.
03
The championship fight remains open behind the leader. AlphaTauri, Engstler, JP-Motorsport, and the Schubert BMW entries remain within reach, making every position valuable as the season approaches its second half.
04
Qualifying could decide the race. With a 100-minute sprint format, starting position carries even more importance. A strong qualifying lap could be the difference between fighting for victory and getting trapped in traffic.
05
Strategy will be critical once again. Pit timing and tire decisions played a major role at Long Beach, and Detroit is expected to create another strategic battle between teams looking for track position.
06
The midfield remains unpredictable. The battle behind the championship leaders continues to tighten, with several teams capable of moving multiple positions in the standings with one strong result.
07
The final sprint before the endurance return. Detroit marks the final short-format race before the championship heads back into endurance racing at Watkins Glen. Teams will be looking to maximize points before the season returns to six-hour competition.

Race Recap

Final Result

PosCarTeamPoints

Walkenhorst Motorsport wins Round 5. After the most turbulent individual race of the season — early contact, two separate stints at the lead, two drops from it, and finally two full hours holding station clean — the win moves them from fourth in points entering the round to the outright championship lead.

Both pre-race title favorites had rough rounds: Engstler finished 12th, its worst result of the season, and AlphaTauri finished 5th after recovering from as low as 13th at halfway. Corvette Racing's #3 had one of the most notable individual arcs of the day — contact damage, repair, full retirement, an unexpected return, and a climb to 8th by race end.

Round 6 · Upcoming — 6 Hour Endurance

Watkins Glen

The endurance grind gives way to the shortest race of the season so far — a tight, unforgiving street circuit with almost no room to recover from a mistake.

The Track

Long Beach is the longest-running major street race in North America, dating back to 1975 — a temporary circuit carved out of the city streets around the old Long Beach Convention Center, running along the waterfront with the signature Shoreline Drive front straight lined by palm trees. The modern layout is 1.968 miles with 11 turns, and it's a genuine dual-purpose venue — host to IndyCar's showcase street race every April, but also a real IMSA SportsCar Championship round in its own right.

The Format Factor

This is the shortest race on the calendar to date, and it changes the entire calculus of the championship fight. Where Daytona and Sebring rewarded teams built for the long haul, Long Beach rewards raw pace and clean execution over a tight window — there's no time to claw back a slow start the way Walkenhorst did across 8 hours at Sebring.

The Contenders

Walkenhorst Motorsport arrives as championship leader for the first time all season, and their form profile suggests Long Beach should suit them — their two strongest results (Laguna Seca, Sebring) both came in formats that rewarded pace over pure survival.

AlphaTauri and JP-Motorsport are the two teams built for exactly this kind of race. Both delivered statement performances at Laguna Seca, and a tight, short street sprint plays directly to whatever gave them that speed.

Engstler and Schubert, tied for 4th, have the most to prove here rather than defend — their season-long identity is exactly what a 100-minute sprint devalues.

TF-Sport and Red Bull, tied at 6th, are the sleeper picks — TF-Sport's best result of the season came at Laguna, the same short-format signal.

Fan Favorites

Walkenhorst

Defending a first-ever points lead, on a format that should suit them.

AlphaTauri

The clearest path back to the championship lead if the short-format speed shows up again.

JP-Motorsport

Quietly built a strong season — a Long Beach win puts them in a real title fight.

TF-Sport

The form guide's dark horse, still flying under the radar.

Prediction

Given the season's already thrown out most pre-race predictions so far, this one comes with a grain of salt — but the format strongly favors AlphaTauri or JP-Motorsport over the two attrition-built teams. Expect Walkenhorst to defend competently rather than dominate, and the real fight for the win to come from further back in the standings than the points leader.

Four rounds in the books after this one, seven still to run — the championship picture should be shaped as much by outright speed as by survival.
